```
const editImage = async (
    originalImage: string,
    description: string,
  ): Promise<string> => {
    // Check if the originalImage is empty or undefined
    if (!originalImage) {
      console.error("No image provided for editing.");
      return ""; // Or handle the error as needed, e.g., return a default image or throw an error
    }

    try {
      const model = new GoogleGenerativeAI(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_GEMINI_API_KEY).getGenerativeModel({ model: "gemini-pro-vision" });
  
      const result = await model.generateContent([
        "You are a helpful image editor. Given an image and user feedback, refine the image details accordingly.  Focus on making the image more accurate and consistent with the provided description. Only return the base64 encoded image.",
        {
          inlineData: {
            mimeType: "image/jpeg",
            data: originalImage.replace(/^data:image\/jpeg;base64,/, ""),
          },
        },
        description,
      ]);

      const response = result.response;
      const text = response.text();

      // Check if the response contains "data:image/jpeg;base64,"
      if (text.startsWith("data:image/jpeg;base64,")) {
        return text; // Return the full string if it's already base64 encoded
      } else {
        // Handle the case where the response is just the base64 string
        return `data:image/jpeg;base64,${text}`;
      }
    } catch (error) {
      console.error("Error editing image with Gemini:", error);
      return originalImage; // Return original if error
    }
  };
```

```javascript
import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
import { useNavigate } from 'react-router-dom';
import { Trash2, Calendar, Loader2 } from 'lucide-react';
import { getSavedGames, deleteGameData } from '../utils/localStorageUtils';

const LoadGameScreen = () => {
  const navigate = useNavigate();
  const [savedGames, setSavedGames] = useState([]);
  const [searchTerm, setSearchTerm] = useState('');
  const [deleteConfirmId, setDeleteConfirmId] = useState(null);
  const [isDeleting, setIsDeleting] = useState(false);
  const [isLoading, setIsLoading] = useState(true);

  useEffect(() => {
    const fetchSavedGames = async () => {
      try {
        const games = await getSavedGames();
        setSavedGames(games || []); // Ensure it's an array, even if null or undefined
      } catch (error) {
        console.error("Error fetching saved games:", error);
      } finally {
        setIsLoading(false);
      }
    };
    fetchSavedGames();
  }, []);

  const handleDeleteSave = (e, id) => {
    e.stopPropagation();
    setDeleteConfirmId(id);
  };

  const confirmDelete = async (id) => {
    setIsDeleting(true);
    try {
      await deleteGameData(id);
      setSavedGames(savedGames.filter(game => game.id !== id));
    } catch (error) {
      console.error("Error deleting game:", error);
    } finally {
      setIsDeleting(false);
      setDeleteConfirmId(null);
    }
  };

  const filteredGames = savedGames.filter(game =>
    game.charNameInput.toLowerCase().includes(searchTerm.toLowerCase())
  );

  return (
    <div className="flex flex-col items-center justify-center h-screen bg-gray-100">
      <h1 className="text-3xl font-bold mb-8">Load Game</h1>
      <div className="w-full max-w-md p-4 bg-white rounded-lg shadow-md">
        <input
          type="text"
          placeholder="Search by character name..."
          className="w-full p-2 mb-4 border rounded"
          value={searchTerm}
          onChange={(e) => setSearchTerm(e.target.value)}
        />

        {isLoading ? (
          <div className="flex justify-center items-center">
            <Loader2 className="animate-spin h-5 w-5 mr-3 text-gray-400" />
            <span className="text-gray-400">Loading saved games...</span>
          </div>
        ) : filteredGames.length === 0 ? (
          <p className="text-gray-500 text-center">No saved stories found.</p>
        ) : (
          filteredGames.map(game => (
            <div key={game.id} className="mb-4">
              {/* Individual game card rendering here */}
            </div>
          ))
        )}
      </div>

      {deleteConfirmId && (
        <div className="fixed inset-0 bg-black bg-opacity-50 flex justify-center items-center z-20">
          <div className="bg-white p-6 rounded-lg shadow-md">
            <p>Are you sure you want to delete this game?</p>
            <div className="flex justify-end mt-4">
              <button
                className="px-4 py-2 mr-2 text-white bg-red-500 rounded hover:bg-red-600 focus:outline-none"
                onClick={() => confirmDelete(deleteConfirmId)}
                disabled={isDeleting}
              >
                {isDeleting ? <Loader2 className="animate-spin h-4 w-4" /> : "Delete"}
              </button>
              <button
                className="px-4 py-2 text-gray-700 bg-gray-200 rounded hover:bg-gray-300 focus:outline-none"
                onClick={() => setDeleteConfirmId(null)}
              >
                Cancel
              </button>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>
      )}
    </div>
  );
};

export default LoadGame

**Optimizing API Usage**

I'm currently focused on analyzing the request and pinpointing the core issue, which is excessive Gemini 2.5 Flash API calls. The goal, as I see it now, is clear: optimize the app to cut down on these calls without sacrificing functionality. **Analyzing Core Components**

I've been examining the different components of the app, focusing on how they generate content: stories, images, and reading analysis. My analysis highlights specific high-cost areas. For example, generating scene summaries involves two calls to Gemini 2.5 Flash, which makes it a priority for optimization. I'm also looking at the impact of image generation and reading score calculation on overall API usage.


**Evaluating Potential Solutions**

I'm currently weighing various solutions to reduce API calls. I'm considering passing the story text directly to the image model, but the potential context issues are a concern. I'm focusing more on prompt optimization and skipping image generation for some chapters. I'm also examining how the current game state can reduce repeated calls. Caching `generateImageTweaks` is another avenue, as they are often generic and don't need constant AI interaction.


**Exploring Alternative Approaches**

I'm currently exploring more optimization strategies. I'm prioritizing `generateSceneImage`, considering a static list of randomized common tweaks. Instead of summarizing the text, I'm thinking about sending the last paragraph and character description directly to the image model. Alternatively, making the scene generation optional or manual is another avenue. Moreover, I'm considering switching from `gemini-3-pro-preview` to `gemini-2.5-flash` for story generation, as it has better limits.


**Streamlining API Interactions**

I'm now zeroing in on optimizing `calculateReadingScore`, which occurs at the end of each chapter. Although essential for AI scoring, I'm ensuring it only activates if the transcript has content. Regarding `generateSceneImage`, I'm switching to a streamlined approach: combining the final text portion with character descriptions for a single API call to the image model. Additionally, I'm swapping the story model to `gemini-2.5-flash` for significant quota benefits. And finally, I've addressed potential double-firing issues in our React components.


**Implementing Revised Logic**

I'm now implementing the code changes to streamline the API calls. I'm focusing on `services/gemini.ts`, modifying `generateStoryStart` and `generateNextChapter` to use `gemini-2.5-flash` for better quota management.  For `generateSceneImage`, I'm removing the summarization step and incorporating the last 1000 characters of the story text directly, along with character descriptions, into the image model prompt.  Furthermore, I've replaced the static list of image tweaks with a random selection from a larger array of ~20 items.


**Completing API Integration**

I'm making progress. I've switched the story generation model to `gemini-2.5-flash` and adjusted the logic for `generateSceneImage` to use raw text and character descriptions. I'm also implementing a local random logic for image tweaks. My primary focus now is implementing and testing the changes in `services/gemini.ts` and updating any call sites, although the interface should remain the same. Finally, I'm polishing the prompts to ensure strict JSON formatting with the smaller model.


**Simplifying Image Generation**

I'm now streamlining the image generation process by eliminating an unnecessary API call. Instead of summarizing the scene text separately, I'm integrating the relevant story context directly into the image model prompt. I'm focusing on ensuring this consolidated approach maintains image quality while reducing overall Flash API usage.

I will optimize the application to significantly reduce API calls and avoid hitting rate limits.

Here is the specification for the changes:

1.  **Switch Story Model**: Change the story generation model from `gemini-3-pro-preview` (which has lower rate limits) to `gemini-2.5-flash`. Flash is faster, much cheaper/generous with the free tier, and capable of handling children's stories effectively.
2.  **Eliminate "Image Tweak" API Calls**: The `generateImageTweaks` function currently calls the AI to ask for suggestions like "Add a hat". I will replace this with a local, randomized list of fun options. This saves 1 API call per new game.
3.  **Optimize Scene Generation (50% reduction)**: Currently, `generateSceneImage` makes **two** calls: one to summarize the text, and a second to generate the image. I will consolidate this into a **single** call by passing the relevant story context directly to the image generation model.

These changes will reduce the API load per chapter by ~50% and move the heavy text generation to a higher-limit tier.

Here is where to find them and what to name them:

    4.  Copy the token (it starts with `hf_`).

as a fallback, could we use pollinations ai and base the image generation on this python code?

import requests
import time

def generate_image_pollinations(prompt):
    # Pollinations uses a simple URL structure
    # You can customize width/height/seed in the URL parameters
    # encode the prompt to be URL-safe
    encoded_prompt = requests.utils.quote(prompt)
    url = f"https://image.pollinations.ai/prompt/{encoded_prompt}?width=1024&height=1024&nologo=true"
    
    response = requests.get(url)
    
    if response.status_code == 200:
        # Save the image binary to a file
        filename = f"image_{int(time.time())}.jpg"
        with open(filename, "wb") as f:
            f.write(response.content)
        return filename
    else:
        print(f"Error: {response.status_code}")
        return None

Fix the following errors:

Failed to execute 'setItem' on 'Storage': Setting the value of 'readquest_saves' exceeded the quota.

**Diagnosing the storage bottleneck**

I've been drilling into the `QuotaExceededError` issue. It's clear that hitting the storage limit stems from the Base64 encoded images ballooning the saved game state. It's a classic `localStorage` limitation: around 5MB. Now, I'm thinking about alternative strategies to store those images.


**Analyzing storage solutions**

I've been exploring IndexedDB and image compression. IndexedDB seems like a great option for the long term. I need to get images stored for a user, as part of their saved game. Currently the images are Base64 encoded, which are large. Changing to `IndexedDB` would be complex, but is the best option for storing large data in the browser. In the short term, though, I can optimize what is stored in localStorage.


**Devising a new storage model**

The `GameState` includes images that blow past `localStorage` limits. A 1024x1024 image easily takes 1-2MB when Base64 encoded. So, I need to use IndexedDB. I'll build a service that handles saving/loading. I'll update `App.tsx` to use this new service, keeping smaller settings in `localStorage`. I'm creating `services/storage.ts` to implement IndexedDB's `put` and `get` operations. I'll see how this looks.


**Adapting to asynchronous storage**

I'm now integrating the async operations of IndexedDB into the main application. This means I'm refactoring `App.tsx`'s `useEffect`, `handleSaveGame`, and `handleDeleteSave` to handle the asynchronous nature of the database interactions.  The save and load logic is being moved to a dedicated `services/db.ts` to encapsulate the IndexedDB interactions, with the primary methods being `saveGame`, `getSavedGames`, and `deleteSave`. This includes defining the `readquest_db` database and `saves` object store in the new service.


**Refining database interactions**

I'm writing the `getSavedGames` function now, which retrieves saved games from IndexedDB. It will return an array of `GameState` objects, handling potential errors during the database query. I'm also implementing a `deleteSave` function to remove saved games. This will involve opening a transaction in `readwrite` mode, deleting the item, and managing success/error cases. The goal is to encapsulate all the necessary IndexedDB interactions within `services/db.ts` for a clean interface.


**Completing the IndexedDB integration**

I'm finalizing the data access layer. I've implemented the `SavedGamesFromDB` and `deleteSaveFromDB` methods, which use promises for asynchronous interaction with the IndexedDB database. The `openDB` helper function is defined to keep the IndexedDB connection centralized. Next, I will replace the current state management with the new methods I just created. Then I will modify `App.tsx` to call `getSavedGamesFromDB` on initial load and update the save and delete operations. This includes handling the asynchronous nature of IndexedDB interactions with the UI.


**Improving migration strategy**

I'm now focusing on migrating existing `localStorage` data to IndexedDB. The plan is to check for `localStorage` data during the component mount and initiate a migration if any is found. I'll need to handle potential errors and ensure the UI updates appropriately after the migration completes. I'm updating `App.tsx`'s `useEffect` to implement this. I also need to make sure I clear out `localStorage` data after successful migration to avoid redundancy and potential confusion. The asynchronous nature of `IndexedDB` needs careful handling in state management and UI updates.


**Completing data migration**

I've implemented the `useEffect` hook to handle migration from `localStorage`. After parsing existing saves, it converts them to IndexedDB format. The function is also designed to sort the saves by last saved date. The UI state is updated after completion, and I also added logic to clear `localStorage` after successful migration. For saving, I've modified `handleSaveGame` to use `saveGameToDB` after construction.


**Implementing the save confirmation**

I'm now integrating the save confirmation message within the `handleSaveGame` function. Upon successful saving, I'll update the UI state to show a confirmation message briefly. I've added a timeout to automatically hide the confirmation after a couple of seconds.  The catch block now properly displays any database save errors in the application.


**Updating asynchronous interactions**

I'm now focusing on integrating the asynchronous changes into the core game logic. `handleSaveGame`, `handleFinishChapter`, `handleMakeChoice`, `confirmDelete`, and the initialization `useEffect` within `App.tsx` have been updated. Ensuring all calls to `handleSaveGame` properly handle its now asynchronous nature is crucial. I've added error handling to manage potential IndexedDB failures. I'll also modify the parent dashboard to use the updated saved game state.


**Debugging the data insertion**

I'm now investigating a specific insertion error within the IndexedDB operation. I'm focusing on why a recent attempt to save data into IndexedDB is still triggering a quota error. It seems like I'm still hitting some storage limit, even after all the previous changes.  I need to go deeper to understand the root cause of this persistent issue.

I will solve the storage quota issue by migrating the save system from `localStorage` (which is limited to ~5MB) to `IndexedDB` (which allows storing hundreds of MBs of data, perfect for images).

I will create a new service helper `services/db.ts` to handle database operations and update `App.tsx` to use this asynchronous storage.

Here are the changes:
1.  **Create `services/db.ts`**: A wrapper for IndexedDB to save, load, and delete game states.
2.  **Update `App.tsx`**:
    *   Replace `localStorage` calls with the new `db` service.
    *   Add a migration script to automatically move existing saves from LocalStorage to IndexedDB on the first run.
    *   Update `handleSaveGame` and `confirmDelete` to be asynchronous.

payload.text: the main character window now contains a connection slow image, having previously had an image inside.

**Analyzing Connection Issues**

I'm currently investigating the "Connection Slow" placeholder appearing where the main character image used to be. My primary suspect is the timeout logic I integrated into `App.tsx`. I need to review the network requests and timings to pinpoint the issue. Perhaps a recent code change has introduced a bottleneck or misconfigured the timeout duration.


**Diagnosing Image Rendering**

I'm now zeroing in on why the image might vanish after initially loading. It seems the component is being remounted, which could cause `character?.imageUrl` to become `null` temporarily. The image URL might be failing validation or timing out during the new check. My analysis now involves tracing the lifecycle of the `character` object and how it interacts with the `useEffect` hook. I need to figure out when this reset occurs.


**Evaluating Base64 Image Loading**

It seems the "Connection Slow" message might stem from an unexpected delay in rendering the base64-encoded image from Cloudflare. While base64 should load instantly, I'm questioning if a large image size could cause a brief hiccup, leading to the timeout. I need to verify that `isImageLoadedRef` is not being prematurely set to false, even with a Base64 string. I'll check how `character.imageUrl` is populated. Perhaps the source isn't always base64, especially when loading from a saved game.


**Addressing URL Expiration**

I'm now focusing on the image source logic. Specifically, I'm examining how saved game data interacts with image URLs. I've realized Pollinations URLs, potentially stored in saved games, have an expiration. This could be the root cause of the connection issues. Also, I'm checking the interaction of Cloudflare's Base64 image generation and the image loading state. If the character is newly generated, it should be Base64, and render almost immediately with no delay.


**Isolating State Reset**

I'm now zeroing in on a potential state reset triggered by calling `handleRegenerateImage`. This call impacts `character`, which then causes a rerender with `useEffect`. Specifically, I'm tracing the transition from a non-Base64 image to the "Connection Slow" placeholder. The image displayed in the sidebar reads from a displaySrc variable, which is a key source to evaluate.


**Correcting Sidebar Loading**

I've pinpointed the root cause! The sidebar's image tag omits the `onLoad` handler. This oversight prevents `isImageLoadedRef.current` from being set to `true`, leading to the timeout and the "Connection Slow" placeholder. I'm implementing the `onLoad={handleImageLoad}` event handler in the sidebar image tag now. This should resolve the issue and guarantee the sidebar displays the character image correctly. I'll test it thoroughly after this change.


**Verifying Image Display**

I'm verifying that adding `onLoad={handleImageLoad}` to the sidebar image resolves the "Connection Slow" issue. It's now clear that without this, `isImageLoadedRef` remains false, triggering the timeout. I believe the image renders correctly for 30 seconds initially because `displaySrc` contains a valid URL. I also need to make sure that the character's object identity remains constant through view changes to ensure the image persists correctly across the application.


**Resolving Image Lifecycle**

I'm now zeroing in on an issue arising specifically when loading a saved game. The `useEffect` hook, dependent on `character?.imageUrl`, doesn't re-run in the 'reading' view, leading to the sidebar image not triggering `handleImageLoad`. I'm going to add `onLoad={handleImageLoad}` to the sidebar image tag in `App.tsx` to force the correct logic. I'm also considering whether the `displaySrc` logic is needed for loaded games. I will verify that the image loads without the `onLoad` handler.


**Implementing Sidebar Updates**

My focus is now on the sidebar image in `App.tsx`. I've added `onLoad={handleImageLoad}` to the `img` tag to address the timeout. I've also swapped `onError` with `handleImageError` to ensure the sidebar image gracefully handles errors consistently with the rest of the app. This is crucial for maintaining the intended UI behavior and state consistency across the application, especially with the `displaySrc` updates.

**Implementing Error Handling**

I'm now focusing on a potential feedback loop: does `handleImageError` triggering the placeholder image cause an infinite rerender? The `useEffect` hook relies on `character.imageUrl`, and the error handler sets `displaySrc` to a placeholder. However, because the `onLoad` fires for placeholder, and the handler does not prevent the timeout, the cycle breaks, but should not cause re-render. To avoid the timeout overwriting the error placeholder, I'll set `isImageLoadedRef.current` to `true` within `handleImageError`. I will apply this directly in `App.tsx`.

I will fix the issue where the character image in the sidebar is replaced by a "Connection Slow" placeholder after 30 seconds. This happens because the sidebar image was missing the signal to tell the application that it had successfully loaded, causing the safety timer to think it failed.

**Changes:**
1.  **`App.tsx`**: Add `onLoad={handleImageLoad}` and update `onError={handleImageError}` to the character image in the sidebar (Reading View). This ensures that when the image loads successfully, the 30-second timeout is cancelled.
